title: payment — model relationships
description: Four owned entities. Payment is the terminal aggregate — it exists only after a successful PaymentIntent. PaymentDispute attaches to Payment. PaymentMethod is standalone (customer-scoped) and referenced by PaymentIntent + Payment. Every outbound FK stays same-tenant per tenancy-columns.md §5. NO application_id / region_id / organization_id / branch_id / scope_node_id on any owned row.

internal.description: Every succeeded PaymentIntent has exactly one Payment. Failed/cancelled intents have zero. Unique index on payments.payment_intent_id enforces the one-to-one.

RESTRICT — the parent intent cannot be hard-deleted while its Payment exists. Payment retention (7y) exceeds Intent retention (90d); the intent is preserved for the payment's lifetime via the RESTRICT.
Zero-or-more disputes per payment. Uncommon but possible (partial disputes, escalations).
RESTRICT — disputes anchor to their payment for audit. Payment retention (7y) exceeds dispute retention window.
A saved method may be used across many intents (repeat customer). Read-side only — new intents can attach the method during their requires_payment_method → requires_confirmation transition.
Nullable — first-time-payer intents attach a fresh method during the flow. RESTRICT once set — historical intents preserve the method reference even after the method soft-deletes.
A saved method may have accumulated many payments over its lifetime. Read-side only.
Nullable — one-time bank transfer / mail-in check / cash-at-counter payments have no saved method. RESTRICT once set.

outbound.description: Every payment is tenant-scoped per tenancy-columns.md §3.
RESTRICT — invoices carrying payments cannot be hard-deleted until retention expires.

RESTRICT once set — the recording double-entry transaction stays for the payment's lifetime. Nullable only during the microsecond window between Payment insert + TransactionRecorder.record within the same DB transaction.

Polymorphic customer: User, Athlete. Each customer_type has its own tenant scoping; PaymentMethodObserver validates that customer.tenant_id == payment_method.tenant_id (defense-in-depth against cross-tenant FK smuggling).
RESTRICT — invoices with active intents cannot be voided until intents terminate.
RESTRICT once set — the refund issued to resolve the dispute is anchored via this FK. Refund module owns the refund row + issues via provider API when POST /disputes/{dispute}/accept is called.
RESTRICT once set — the chargeback that this dispute escalated into (via EscalateDisputeToChargebackJob after status='lost'). Chargeback module owns the chargeback row + regulator-facing lifecycle.

inbound.description: Payment module rows are referenced by three downstream modules (planned consumers):

examples.reference: refunds.payment_id → payments.id (RESTRICT). Refunds attach to their originating payment. amount_refunded_cents on payments is accumulated by refund events.
examples.cascade: payment soft-delete refused when has open refunds; payment hard-purge only after retention window closes AND all refunds retention-purged

examples.reference: chargebacks.payment_id → payments.id (RESTRICT). Chargebacks attach to their originating payment. May also link back to payment_disputes.chargeback_id.
examples.cascade: payment cannot be hard-purged while open chargebacks exist

examples.reference: No FK. Growth reads Payment via events (MarketingEventCapturer subscribes to PaymentCaptured domain event via #[AsDomainEventMapper]). Marketing rows are self-contained; they never hold a FK on Payment.

morphs.description: Polymorphic customer of the saved payment method. User = the account owner. Athlete = the parent-managed minor (parent's UI attaches a method on the athlete's profile via delegation). Each supported type is tenant-scoped in its own module. The observer validates cross-model tenant integrity on write.

notes: None of the four owned rows carry `application_id` — cascades through tenant_id → tenants.application_id per tenancy-columns.md §2. Only the 8 named rows carry application_id directly; payment_* is not among them.
None of the four owned rows carry `region_id`, `organization_id`, `branch_id`, or `scope_node_id`. Payment is a domain-plane concern — the growth/scope substrate does not apply.

Payment.transaction_id may be transiently NULL during the atomic Payment.create → TransactionRecorder.record sequence (same DB transaction). Query paths that need transaction MUST either JOIN with FOR SHARE or query after commit.
Payment.payment_method_id is nullable — one-time flows (bank_transfer, cash, mail-in-check) do not create a saved PaymentMethod row.
The one-to-one from PaymentIntent → Payment is enforced by BOTH the unique index on payments.payment_intent_id AND the PaymentObserver.creating check (defense-in-depth).

PaymentDispute.refund_id + PaymentDispute.chargeback_id are mutually exclusive at any given time — dispute either escalated to Chargeback OR was resolved by accepting + refunding, never both. Enforced by observer.

The morph on PaymentMethod.customer is loose — the module's PaymentMethodObserver enforces the customer_type is in the supportedTypes list, but the schema allows any (customer_type, customer_id) pair for extensibility.

The payment module NEVER holds a payment_provider_configs table — provider configs are owned by whichever module supplies the credentials (typically a shared payment-providers config, hydrated at boot). Payment refers to provider by enum slug only + reads credentials via the compliance/encryption service.
